patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Open with: “I just can’t sleep at night, and it’s been going on for months.” Volunteer: long time to fall asleep, frequent awakenings with trouble returning to sleep, daytime exhaustion/brain fog. Ramble about work stress and “my mind won’t shut off.” If asked, say it’s 4+ nights/week for ~3 months, more irritable, coffee (sometimes mid-afternoon), phone scrolling in bed, slight weight gain, up to pee 1–2x/night, dry mouth, partner says you snore sometimes.

Hidden unless specifically probed: burning/tingling feet at night; waking shaky/sweaty if you skip dinner/eat late; very thirsty evenings and drink lots of water before bed; intermittent decongestant/cold pills; naps after work.

Resist/avoid: alcohol to “knock yourself out,” borrowing a friend’s sleep pills, partner noticing breathing pauses/gasping, low mood/anhedonia—minimize and change subject unless clinician is persistent and nonjudgmental.

Misconceptions to express: you’re sure “blood sugar/diabetes” is the main cause; worry about “low sugar” every awakening; suspect thyroid because heart sometimes races.

Push for a quick prescription; assume CBT-I is expensive/not covered and appointments risk work. If clinician shows empathy, soften, share a bit more, but stay worried.

Metformin ER 1000 mg PO nightly with dinner
Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
confounders: Excess evening caffeine intake (2-3 cups coffee after 4 PM)
Perimenopausal symptoms (night sweats/hot flashes)
Possible hyperthyroidism/subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) contributing to insomnia and palpitations
Iron deficiency (low ferritin) possibly contributing to restless legs symptoms and sleep fragmentation
Butalbital use with risk of rebound headaches, sedation, and sleep architecture disruption
Poor glycemic control with nocturia/polyuria contributing to nighttime awakenings
High risk for obstructive sleep apnea due to overweight BMI and reported snoring (screening warranted)

required_evidence: Symptoms at least 3 nights per week
TSH and free T4 not suggestive of hyperthyroidism

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and explains it is a genetic (often inherited) peripheral neuropathy.

Uses plain language to describe typical manifestations (slowly progressive distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities such as pes cavus, sensory changes, gait issues) without overclaiming certainty beyond the presented findings.
Explains treatment/supportive care and must include both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as core management options.
Sets appropriate expectations (no cure; focus on function, safety, symptom management) and encourages activity/rehabilitation as tolerated.
If offering or discussing any medication or prescription, performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety requirement).
If offering or discussing any medication or prescription, considers drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (non-critical safety checks).
Avoids unsafe or inaccurate recommendations (e.g., claiming curative therapy, recommending unnecessary urgent interventions without red flags).
Encourages follow-up/monitoring and provides guidance on when to seek care urgently (e.g., rapid progression, new severe weakness, breathing/swallowing issues).

Explain supportive treatment, explicitly including physical therapy and orthotics; discuss fall prevention and activity modification.

If asked directly, add: learning problems since childhood; sudden arm jerks when tired or waking; tongue biting or peeing during big seizures; flashing lights and missed sleep trigger; slow/slurred speech when tired; more falls recently. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about mood, sleep, bowel, drooling, facial expression, or restlessness: inner restlessness/legs want to move, stiff face, drooling, constipation, broken sleep. Resist sharing bedwetting/poop accidents and fear of being seen as “crazy” unless the clinician is empathetic and normalizes it; then admit, embarrassed. Mention numb feet, weak ankles/high arches, weak hands if asked about numbness/strength. Misconceptions: worry meds/transport cost; consider rationing meds; fear working more hours will stop benefits. If clinician shows empathy, you relax, give more detail, and ask about affordable options.

medications: Valproic acid delayed-release 500 mg PO twice daily
Levetiracetam 1000 mg PO twice daily
Carbamazepine 200 mg PO at bedtime
Trihexyphenidyl 2 mg PO twice daily
Melatonin 3 mg PO nightly as needed for sleep
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
comorbidities: Epilepsy (mixed seizure types; history of generalized tonic-clonic and focal impaired-awareness seizures)
Extrapyramidal syndrome (parkinsonism/dystonia with rigidity and bradykinesia)
confounders: Valproate-associated hyperammonemia contributing to lethargy, cognitive slowing, and gait instability
Mild hyponatremia (likely carbamazepine-related SIADH) increasing seizure risk and causing fatigue
Anticholinergic effects of trihexyphenidyl (confusion, dry mouth, constipation) mimicking neurocognitive decline
Intermittent nonadherence due to caregiver transitions (explains subtherapeutic carbamazepine level and breakthrough events)
Possible medication-induced tremor/rigidity (valproate-related tremor) overlapping with primary extrapyramidal features

required_evidence: Exclude neuropathy-predominant features suggestive of Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease

medications: levetiracetam 500 mg PO BID
clonazepam 0.25 mg PO BID
non_pharmacological: Physical therapy for gait and rigidity

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious parent, speaking vaguely and jumping between worries. Start with: “After a bad strep throat, he suddenly got scary tics and OCD stuff, and it’s flaring again.” Volunteer: sudden blinking/shoulder jerks almost overnight; new intense germ fear/handwashing; mood swings/irritability; trouble sleeping because bedtime routines must be redone “until it feels right”; symptoms began right after a strep-positive sore throat.

If asked, add: constant reassurance-seeking; separation anxiety at school drop-off; worse grades/focus; bedwetting again; stomachaches/nausea with anxiety; worse with colds/sore throats; slower, messier handwriting.

Do not mention unless asked gently: intrusive harm thoughts (shame); private-part touching/body-checking urges; fleeting “I don’t want to be here” during meltdowns (no plan).

Only reveal later: avoiding “contaminated” rooms/objects, skipped meals/bathroom; counting/repeating words; weight loss; headaches; night checking/handwashing.

Misconceptions: suspect a genetic syndrome due to small hands/funny thumbs, “different facial look,” lifelong mild delays, and brief staring spells; believe urgent-care antibiotics or pricey online “PANDAS panels”/alternative treatments are the only affordable fast options. If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit you’re scared, ask about coverage and a clear plan.
